original_hypothesis.title: VoteWeighting Removed Nominee's changesSum Entries Persist and Distort Future Slope Adjustments
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: When removeNominee is called, it does not clean up the changesWeight and changesSum entries scheduled at future lock expiration times of users who voted for the removed nominee. If users do not voluntarily call revokeRemovedNomineeVotingPower, these scheduled slope decrements will fire at the original lock expiration times, incorrectly reducing the global sum's slope and further distorting weight calculations.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: Users have no obligation or incentive to revoke votes from removed nominees. The protocol relies on user cooperation for cleanup, but game theory suggests many users will not bother. Each unrevoked vote creates a scheduled future corruption point in changesSum.

proof_of_signal.pattern: removeNominee does not iterate over voters to clean changesSum/changesWeight at their lock expiration times

preconditions: Nominee is removed while multiple users have active votes directed to it
Lock expirations of those users arrive in the future

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: No cleanup of changesSum entries at voter lock expiration times during nominee removal
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: changesSum[lockEnd] will still decrement slope at lock expiration even though the nominee was removed, causing double-counting of slope reduction
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Protocol relies on voluntary user action (revokeRemovedNomineeVotingPower) for cleanup

analysis_questions: What percentage of users are likely to call revokeRemovedNomineeVotingPower?
How significant is the slope distortion from unrevoked votes?
Can this compound with TECH-H001 to accelerate global sum corruption?
validation_requirements: Remove nominee with 5+ voters, wait for lock expirations without revocation
Measure pointsSum slope errors after lock expirations fire
Compare nomineeRelativeWeight accuracy before and after

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: Popular nominee removed by admin. Most voters don't revoke. When their veOLAS locks expire, changesSum incorrectly decrements the global slope for weight that was already removed. This causes pointsSum to undercount, further distorting relative weights for all remaining nominees.
prerequisites: Nominee removal with multiple active voters

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Progressive distortion of staking incentive distribution across all nominees
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All active staking nominees and their stakers
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Partially recoverable if all users revoke, but practically unlikely

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: No test for changesSum consistency after nominee removal without user revocation

technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Fires at user lock expiration times
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable by monitoring pointsSum.slope vs sum of individual nominee slopes

validation_result.mechanism: Incomplete state cleanup on nominee removal
validation_result.impact_summary: When removeNominee is called, the slope contribution of the removed nominee's voters persists in pointsSum and changesSum entries remain scheduled at lock expirations. This causes the global weight sum to decay too fast and then be further decremented, distorting relative weight calculations for all remaining nominees.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The removeNominee function zeroes the nominee's bias via pointsWeight[nomineeHash][nextTime].bias = 0 and adjusts pointsSum[nextTime].bias accordingly, but critically does NOT: (1) remove the nominee's slope contribution from pointsSum[nextTime].slope, and (2) clean up changesSum entries at voter lock expiration times. This creates a two-phase distortion: first, the excess slope causes the global sum's bias to decay faster than it should between removal and lock expirations; second, when changesSum[lockEnd] fires at each voter's lock expiration, it further decrements the slope from a sum that was already being over-decayed. The revokeRemovedNomineeVotingPower function exists as a voluntary cleanup mechanism, but relies entirely on users proactively calling it, which game theory suggests many will not do (no direct financial incentive to do so). The net result is that pointsSum will consistently undercount, inflating the relative weights of remaining nominees and distorting the entire emission distribution system.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The removeNominee function in VoteWeighting.sol explicitly sets pointsWeight[nomineeHash][nextTime].bias = 0 and pointsSum[nextTime].bias = newSum, but contains zero lines adjusting pointsSum[nextTime].slope or iterating over changesSum entries. In contrast, when voteForNomineeWeights is called, it adds to both changesWeight[nomineeHash][newSlope.end] and changesSum[newSlope.end]. The _getSum() function iterates week-over-week applying pt.slope -= changesSum[t], so any uncleared changesSum entries will fire and reduce the slope at the scheduled time regardless of nominee removal status.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: Only pointsWeight bias and pointsSum bias are adjusted. pointsSum[nextTime].slope is NOT adjusted to remove the removed nominee's voters' slope contributions. changesWeight and changesSum entries at voter lock expiration times are NOT cleaned up. This means the global sum's slope remains inflated by the removed nominee's voters' slope, causing faster-than-correct bias decay, and changesSum entries will fire at lock expirations further reducing slope from an already distorted sum.

When voting, slope changes are scheduled at the voter's lock end time in both changesWeight and changesSum. These entries persist even after the nominee is removed, and are never cleaned up by removeNominee.

_getSum applies changesSum[t] at each week boundary. Uncleared changesSum entries from voters of removed nominees will fire here, further decrementing slope from a sum that was already being over-decayed due to the removed nominee's slope not being cleared from pointsSum.

This function correctly cleans up changesSum and changesWeight entries, but requires each individual voter to call it voluntarily. The protocol relies on user cooperation for cleanup that is essential to maintaining correct global state.

calling_sequence: 1. User A votes for nominee N with weight W: voteForNomineeWeights schedules changesSum[lockEnd] += slope and adds slope to pointsSum[nextTime].slope
2. User B also votes for nominee N: similar entries added to changesSum and pointsSum
3. Owner calls removeNominee(N): sets pointsWeight[N][nextTime].bias = 0, adjusts pointsSum[nextTime].bias, but does NOT adjust pointsSum[nextTime].slope and does NOT clean changesSum entries
4. _getSum() is called subsequently: applies pt.slope (which still includes removed nominee's voters' slopes) to decay bias each week, causing over-decay
5. When User A's lock expires at lockEnd: changesSum[lockEnd] fires in _getSum(), further reducing slope from the already distorted sum
6. nomineeRelativeWeight calculations for all remaining nominees use the distorted pointsSum, producing incorrect relative weights
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The defect path starts at removeNominee where cleanup is incomplete. The function at VoteWeighting.sol sets pointsWeight bias to 0 and adjusts pointsSum bias but leaves pointsSum slope and changesSum entries untouched. When _getSum() iterates (called by checkpointNominee, nomineeRelativeWeightWrite, or voteForNomineeWeights), it applies the excess slope each week (pt.bias -= pt.slope * WEEK where pt.slope is too high), AND applies changesSum[t] decrements at lock expirations (pt.slope -= changesSum[t]), both contributing to progressive distortion. The Dispenser.calculateStakingIncentives function calls nomineeRelativeWeight which uses pointsSum[t].bias as totalSum, so distorted values directly affect OLAS emission distribution.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: The scenario is realistic: (1) A popular nominee with multiple voters is removed by admin. (2) Voters have no financial incentive to call revokeRemovedNomineeVotingPower - they gain nothing from doing so. (3) The global pointsSum slope remains inflated by the removed nominee's voters' slopes, causing the sum's bias to decrease too fast. (4) At each voter's lock expiration, changesSum fires and further decrements the slope. (5) All nomineeRelativeWeight calculations use the distorted pointsSum, meaning remaining nominees receive distorted emission allocations. This does not require any malicious action - it happens naturally when users simply don't bother to clean up their votes.
validation_result.impact_assessment: The impact is progressive distortion of the global weight sum used to calculate all nominee relative weights. Since pointsSum.bias directly determines the denominator in the relative weight formula (weight = 1e18 * nomineeWeight / totalSum), an artificially low totalSum inflates all remaining nominees' relative weights. This affects every call to Dispenser.calculateStakingIncentives, which determines OLAS emission distribution across all staking targets. Over time, as more nominees are removed without full voter revocation, the distortion compounds. The severity depends on the proportion of voting power directed to removed nominees vs. total voting power.
recommended_mitigations: Modify removeNominee to also zero pointsSum[nextTime].slope by subtracting the removed nominee's slope contribution (available from pointsWeight[nomineeHash][nextTime].slope after _getWeight checkpoint)
Consider an incentive mechanism (e.g., small gas rebate or reward) to encourage users to call revokeRemovedNomineeVotingPower
Add a keeper/bot that monitors removed nominees and calls revokeRemovedNomineeVotingPower on behalf of voters (would need architectural changes to support this)
Track voters per nominee to enable admin-triggered batch revocation (gas-intensive but comprehensive)
At minimum, document the limitation clearly and monitor pointsSum accuracy vs. sum of individual nominee weights
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy VoteWeighting with a veOLAS mock. 2. Add a nominee N. 3. Have 5 users lock veOLAS with different lock end times and vote 100% for nominee N. 4. Add another nominee M and have one user vote for M. 5. Record pointsSum values. 6. Call removeNominee(N). 7. Advance time past each voter's lock expiration without calling revokeRemovedNomineeVotingPower. 8. Call checkpoint() or checkpointNominee() to trigger _getSum(). 9. Compare pointsSum.bias and pointsSum.slope against expected values (should only reflect nominee M's votes). 10. Verify that nomineeRelativeWeight for M returns distorted values compared to the correct 100%.
uncertainties: The practical severity depends on what proportion of total veOLAS voting power is directed to removed nominees - if most power is on active nominees, the distortion may be small in practice
The bias floor mechanism (pt.bias = 0, pt.slope = 0 when pt.bias <= dBias) in _getSum provides some natural floor that prevents infinite distortion
It is unknown how frequently nominees are removed in practice and how responsive users would be to revocation calls
additional_testing_needed: Quantitative simulation of distortion magnitude with realistic voting distributions
Test interaction with the related slope bug (pointsSum slope not being adjusted in removeNominee)
Test cumulative effect of multiple nominee removals without revocation
Verify that the bias floor mechanism in _getSum prevents underflow but confirm the distortion before reaching the floor
